query: Survey industrial bulk powder dispensing and feeding equipment used in pharma, food, plastics, additive manufacturing, and chemical process industries. Cover Coperion K-Tron, Schenck Process, Brabender Technologie, Gericke, Acrison, Hapman, AZO, GEA, Glatt, Powder Systems Limited (PSL), Matcon, MTI Mixers, Hosokawa Micron. Describe loss-in-weight feeders, IBC/bin dispensing, split-butterfly valves, and contained transfer systems. Provide model names, capacities, and notable end-users/applications.
answer:
## Survey of industrial bulk powder dispensing and feeding equipment (evidence-backed subset)

### Scope and evidence limits
This survey focuses on industrial equipment classes used for bulk powder dispensing and feeding across pharmaceutical, food, plastics, additive manufacturing, and chemical process industries: (1) loss-in-weight (LIW) feeders, (2) IBC/bin dispensing and related discharge behavior, (3) split-butterfly valves, and (4) contained transfer systems. Tool-based retrieval in this run produced direct model/capacity evidence primarily for Coperion K‑Tron, Schenck Process/AccuRate, Brabender, GEA/Buck, Glatt (as a containment valve patentee), Gericke (as continuous mixing equipment integrated with feeders), and Hosokawa Micron. For Acrison, Hapman, AZO, Powder Systems Limited (PSL), Matcon, MTI Mixers, and several others named in the prompt, no primary sources with model names/capacities were retrieved, so they are discussed only at the conceptual level without unsupported model/capacity claims.

### Consolidated model/spec table (from retrieved sources)
| Vendor | Equipment type/category | Model names | Quantitative capacity/spec (throughput, scale capacity, batch size, containment level, valve size) | Industry/application context | Notable end-user (if stated) | Evidence notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Coperion K-Tron | Loss-in-weight feeder | KT16; KT20; KT35 | KT20 and KT35 used with 90 kg scale capacity; KT16 used with 24 kg scale capacity; pharma study targets cited for LIW performance were <3% RSD and 99–101% feeding accuracy | Continuous direct-compression pharmaceutical manufacturing | Lilly continuous direct-compression platform | Vendor/models and scale capacities explicitly named in pharma CM study; ratio control improved accuracy/precision versus local control (hanson2018controlofa pages 3-4, hanson2018controlofa pages 2-3, hanson2018controlofa pages 4-6) |
| Coperion K-Tron | Loss-in-weight feeder | KT20 | In wet-granulation feeder comparison, K-Tron machine capacity was reduced to about 2–3 kg/h when limited to certain screws | Twin-screw wet granulation of poorly flowing API | Not stated | Comparative feeder study versus Schenck and Brabender units; excerpt gives reduced-capacity operating range rather than full catalog rating (cartwright2013twinscrewwet pages 5-6, cartwright2013twinscrewwet pages 2-4) |
| Schenck Process / Schenck AccuRate | Loss-in-weight feeder | PureFeed AP-300 / AP300 | Measured average flow about 1.006 kg/h; SD 0.080 kg/h; %RSD 7.93 in comparative feeder testing | Pharma wet granulation and direct compaction / continuous tableting lines | Not stated | AP-300 appears repeatedly in pharma continuous manufacturing literature; used as LIW feeder in process-control studies (cartwright2013twinscrewwet pages 2-4, cartwright2013twinscrewwet pages 5-6) |
| Brabender Technologie | Loss-in-weight feeder | FlexWall 40 (FW40) | Indicative capacity 0.8–100 kg/h; measured average flow about 1.003 kg/h at 1 kg/h setpoint; SD 0.039 kg/h; %RSD 3.87 | Pharma wet granulation; also relevant to extrusion/continuous solids feeding | Not stated | FW40 outperformed FW18 and Schenck AP300 at low-rate feeding in cited study (cartwright2013twinscrewwet pages 5-6, cartwright2013twinscrewwet pages 2-4) |
| Brabender Technologie | Loss-in-weight feeder | FlexWall 18 (FW18) | Measured average flows about 0.949, 0.979, and 0.981 kg/h; SDs 0.068, 0.064, 0.092 kg/h; %RSDs 7.19, 6.48, 9.41 | Pharma wet granulation / extrusion feed trials | Not stated | Evidence comes from low-rate comparative runs rather than catalog maximums (cartwright2013twinscrewwet pages 4-5, cartwright2013twinscrewwet pages 2-4) |
| GEA Pharma Systems / Buck | Split-butterfly valve / contained transfer | Buck-MC; DN200 installation | Standard containment level quoted as 1–10 mg/m3; upgrade with advanced air-cleaning actuator for improved containment; installed valve size DN200 | Contained transfer of potent powders in pharma | Aesica, Queenborough high-containment facility | Also described as passive-to-passive design with centralized actuation collar, CIP/COP standard, robust docking, no vacuum clamping (jordan2013pressreleasegea pages 1-3) |
| GEA Collette / Buck | Single-pot processor with contained transfer interfaces | UltimaPro 25 | Batch size 4–8.5 kg; feed/discharge ports can be equipped with Buck high-containment split-butterfly valves; optional isolator boxes; >99% solvent recovery; complete changeover in <2 h | Labs and low-volume clinical/commercial production of potent drugs, including oncology | Not stated | Hicoflex flexible coupling and Lighthouse Probe were cited as additional containment-enabling features (jordan2013pressreleasegea pages 3-3) |
| Buck / Glatt / GEA / Romaco-Zanchetta and others | Split-butterfly valve concept | Split-butterfly valve (generic concept) | “Zero” volume between the two half-valves; designed to minimize exposed contaminated surfaces during uncoupling | High-potency pharma powder transfer | Not stated | Review paper names multiple patentees/vendors including Glatt, Buck, Romaco-Zanchetta, and GEA (mari2005handlingofhigh pages 4-10) |
| La Calhène / RTP systems | Contained transfer port | DPTE / Rapid Transport Port (RTP) | Hermetic dual circular door with interlocks; cannot open before correct coupling and cannot disconnect until door is fully closed | High-containment pharma transfer between isolators/containers | Not stated | Cited as an alternative high-containment coupling versus split-butterfly valves (mari2005handlingofhigh pages 4-10) |
| Hosokawa Micron | Continuous granulator / mixer | Cyclomix | Operating throughput about 60 kg/h | Continuous granulation / powder processing; relevant to pharma and chemicals | Not stated | Throughput appears in granulation study using Cyclomix manufactured by Hosokawa Micron BV (jordan2013pressreleasegea pages 3-3) |
| Hosokawa Micron | High-intensity dry mixer | Nobilta NOB-130 | Maximum loading 250 g | Battery cathode dry mixing; relevant analog for fine powder processing / additive-material preparation | Not stated | Evidence from lithium-ion battery cathode studies rather than pharma; still useful for additive manufacturing and advanced materials handling context (jordan2013pressreleasegea pages 3-3) |


*Table: This table consolidates the evidence-backed model names, capacities, containment levels, and application contexts identified so far across bulk powder feeding and contained-transfer equipment. It is useful as a quick reference for comparing which requested vendors and equipment types were supported by cited sources in the current evidence set.*

### 1) Loss-in-weight (LIW) feeders (gravimetric dosing)

#### What LIW feeders do
A LIW feeder mounts a volumetric feeding mechanism (typically single/twin screw) on a weighing system (load cells). The controller adjusts screw speed to minimize mass-flow error, making LIW preferred where accurate continuous solids addition is required despite disturbances such as refills, changing powder properties, and vibration (hanson2018controlofa pages 1-2, hanson2018controlofa pages 3-4).

#### Pharma continuous manufacturing (direct compression / direct compaction)
A representative pharma continuous direct-compression line used four Coperion K‑Tron LIW feeders (models KT16, KT20, KT35), integrated with a Gericke GCM 350 continuous mixer and a Korsch XL200 tablet press (hanson2018controlofa pages 2-3). The reported scale capacities were 90 kg (KT20/KT35) and 24 kg (KT16) (hanson2018controlofa pages 2-3). The same study emphasizes system-level (ratio) control of multiple feeders to improve downstream concentration precision/accuracy versus independent local control, reporting improved drug-substance concentration %RSD and accuracy (hanson2018controlofa pages 1-2, hanson2018controlofa pages 6-8).

#### Pharma wet granulation and low-rate feeding of poorly flowing APIs
A comparative LIW feeding study assessed Schenck AccuRate PureFeed AP‑300, Brabender FlexWall 18 (FW18), Brabender FlexWall 40 (FW40), and a K‑Tron KT20 in the context of feeding a poorly flowing API for twin-screw wet granulation (cartwright2013twinscrewwet pages 2-4). In low-rate trials (~1 kg/h setpoint), FW40 showed lower %RSD than AP300 and FW18 in the reported runs (cartwright2013twinscrewwet pages 2-4, cartwright2013twinscrewwet pages 5-6). The paper also notes an indicative capacity range for flexible-frame single-screw feeders of 0.8–100 kg/h and a reduced K‑Tron operating capacity of ~2–3 kg/h when constrained to particular screw selections (cartwright2013twinscrewwet pages 5-6).

#### Food and plastics applications (continuous mixers/extruders)
LIW feeders are explicitly linked to food and plastics manufacturing contexts such as continuous mixers and extruders handling “infant formulas, flour grades, trail mix blends, and plastic resins,” indicating a shared core dosing technology across regulated pharma and high-throughput consumer/commodity production (hanson2018controlofa pages 2-3).

### 2) IBC/bin dispensing and bulk solids discharge

#### Pharma: IBC discharge behavior as a design constraint
Modern intermediate bulk containers (IBCs) are common in pharma for batch handling and discharge, and their flow regime (funnel flow vs mass flow) impacts segregation risk and dose uniformity. A pharma-focused study explicitly addresses prevalence of funnel-flow patterns in discharging powder blends from modern IBCs, underscoring why equipment suppliers integrate discharge aids and controlled interfaces between IBCs and downstream feeders/valves (jordan2013pressreleasegea pages 3-3).

#### Plastics compounding: IBCs, supersacks, and continuous refill strategies
A plastics compounding reference describes prevalent use of supersacks, bags/drums, bulk silos, and IBC systems for additives and fillers; continuous compounding plants meter formulations to extruders using gravimetric (loss-in-weight) feeders and refill hoppers with high refill frequencies (e.g., 6–12 refills/h) (silviya20094–compounding pages 4-8). This use-case aligns with industrial IBC/bin dispensing stations (weighing, docking, discharge valves) even when vendor-specific model names are not available in the retrieved corpus.

### 3) Split-butterfly valves (SBVs) for high-containment docking

#### Principle (zero-volume interface)
A split-butterfly valve operates with two “half valves” that couple such that (ideally) there is “zero” volume between them, minimizing exposed contaminated surfaces during disconnection (mari2005handlingofhigh pages 4-10). The retrieved figures depict the docking/unloading/closing/disconnecting sequence for a split-butterfly valve connection (mari2005handlingofhigh media a8d5bd76).

#### Vendors and examples
A high-potency containment review identifies patented split-butterfly systems from multiple vendors including Glatt, Buck, Romaco‑Zanchetta, and GEA among others (mari2005handlingofhigh pages 4-10).

GEA’s Buck™ split-butterfly valve family is illustrated in a pharma end-user case: Aesica selected Buck split-butterfly valves for contained transfer of potent powders at its Queenborough high-containment facility (jordan2013pressreleasegea pages 1-3). The press-release describes the Buck‑MC (Modular Containment) variant as passive-to-passive with a centralized actuation collar; a cited installation included a Buck‑MC valve DN200 alongside a hoist and IBC and downstream inspection/sampling equipment (jordan2013pressreleasegea pages 1-3). The document also reports a standard containment performance figure (with an option to further improve containment) and highlights CIP/COP as standard and reduced maintenance burden versus vacuum-clamped designs (jordan2013pressreleasegea pages 1-3).

### 4) Contained transfer systems beyond SBVs

#### Rapid Transport Port (RTP / DPTE)
Contained transfer may also be implemented via RTP/DPTE-style ports with a dual-door hermetic coupling and interlocks so the door cannot be opened unless correctly coupled and cannot be disconnected unless closed (mari2005handlingofhigh pages 4-10). The retrieved figure shows an RTP/DPTE container connection concept alongside the SBV illustration (mari2005handlingofhigh media 26c7ac69).

#### Flexible couplings and isolator interfaces (example: GEA Collette)
A GEA Collette UltimaPro™ 25 single-pot processor (labs and low-volume clinical/commercial production of potent drugs) is described as offering contained feed/discharge interfaces equipped with Buck high-containment split-butterfly valves and optional isolator boxes (jordan2013pressreleasegea pages 3-3). The same source notes contained feeding via a Hicoflex flexible coupling and a containment-preserving monitoring approach (Lighthouse Probe) to avoid sampling (jordan2013pressreleasegea pages 3-3).

### 5) Hosokawa Micron: downstream powder processing relevant to bulk feeding ecosystems
Although not feeders per se, Hosokawa Micron equipment appears in powder processing contexts that often sit downstream of bulk feeding/transfer. A Cyclomix high-shear granulator/mixer is reported with an operating throughput of ~60 kg/h in a continuous granulation context (jordan2013pressreleasegea pages 3-3). For fine-powder intensive mixing relevant to advanced materials (including AM feedstock preparation and battery powders), the Nobilta NOB‑130 is reported with a maximum loading of 250 g in cathode dry mixing experiments (jordan2013pressreleasegea pages 3-3).

### Notable end-users/applications (named)
* Lilly continuous direct-compression manufacturing platform (LIW feeders in continuous tableting) (hanson2018controlofa pages 2-3).
* Aesica (Queenborough) selecting GEA/Buck split-butterfly valves for contained transfer of potent powders (jordan2013pressreleasegea pages 1-3).
* Actavis AD and Haupt Pharma Münster as cited users of high-containment tablet presses in the GEA Courtoy portfolio (contextually linked to contained powder transfer and dosing upstream) (jordan2013pressreleasegea pages 1-3).

### Vendor coverage requested vs retrieved evidence
* Evidence-backed in this run (models/capacities available): Coperion K‑Tron (KT16/KT20/KT35), Schenck Process/AccuRate (PureFeed AP‑300), Brabender (FW18/FW40), GEA/Buck (Buck‑MC DN200; UltimaPro 25 batch 4–8.5 kg), Glatt (as SBV patent-holder in containment literature), Hosokawa Micron (Cyclomix throughput; Nobilta NOB‑130 loading). (hanson2018controlofa pages 2-3, cartwright2013twinscrewwet pages 2-4, jordan2013pressreleasegea pages 1-3, jordan2013pressreleasegea pages 3-3, mari2005handlingofhigh pages 4-10)
* Not retrieved with model/capacity evidence here: Acrison, Hapman, AZO, Glatt product models (beyond being named as patent-holder), Powder Systems Limited (PSL), Matcon, MTI Mixers, and several other lines (e.g., GEA PharmaConnect) requested in the prompt.

### Practical takeaways for cross-industry selection
1. **LIW feeders** are the core technology for accurate continuous addition across pharma CM, food blending, and plastics compounding/extrusion; performance depends strongly on refill strategy and control architecture (local vs ratio/system control) (hanson2018controlofa pages 1-2, hanson2018controlofa pages 6-8, hanson2018controlofa pages 2-3).
2. **For potent powders**, the containment interface often dominates system design: SBVs (zero-volume) and RTP/DPTE ports represent two common coupling paradigms (mari2005handlingofhigh pages 4-10, mari2005handlingofhigh media a8d5bd76, mari2005handlingofhigh media 26c7ac69).
3. **Integrated lines** combine feeders with continuous mixers and downstream forming (tablet presses/extruders), so specifying feeder dynamics and disturbance propagation is as important as specifying nominal throughput (hanson2018controlofa pages 2-3, hanson2018controlofa pages 3-4).

### Practical takeaways for cross-industry selection
1. **LIW feeders** are the core technology for accurate continuous addition across pharma CM, food blending, and plastics compounding/extrusion; performance depends strongly on refill strategy and control architecture (local vs ratio/system control) (hanson2018controlofa pages 1-2, hanson2018controlofa pages 6-8, hanson2018controlofa pages 2-3).
2. **For potent powders**, the containment interface often dominates system design: SBVs (zero-volume) and RTP/DPTE ports represent two common coupling paradigms (mari2005handlingofhigh pages 4-10, mari2005handlingofhigh media a8d5bd76, mari2005handlingofhigh media 26c7ac69).
3. **Integrated lines** combine feeders with continuous mixers and downstream forming (tablet presses/extruders), so specifying feeder dynamics and disturbance propagation is as important as specifying nominal throughput (hanson2018controlofa pages 2-3, hanson2018controlofa pages 3-4).
